basicInfo.favoriteActivities: NCC drills, team sports, volunteering, structured leadership roles
basicInfo.topicsCuriousAbout: Defense strategy, discipline, public service, national leadership
basicInfo.personalStrengths: Consistency, calm under pressure, stamina, team accountability
basicInfo.avoidsOrDislikes: Chaotic planning, unclear responsibility, repeated excuses

analysis.summary: Aarav shows strong signs of Discipline and Leadership. The profile suggests a strong fit for structured, duty-driven careers that reward resilience and accountability.
analysis.characterReadout: This demo student profile reflects a school-linked learner who performs best when expectations are clear, the mission is meaningful, and personal responsibility is high.
workPreferences: Responds well to structure, routines, and clearly defined responsibility.
Can stay composed in active environments where teamwork and duty matter.
Performs better when goals feel mission-driven rather than purely academic.

interestSignals.reason: The profile strongly leans toward service, defense, and justice-driven responsibility.
Repeated interest in leadership and national service appears across the student basics.
Active routines and stamina-based activities show up consistently.
Peer support and leadership suggest some mentoring potential.
Strategic thinking and evidence-based decisions show moderate interest signals.
Technology is not a central motivation in this demo profile.

basicInfo.favoriteActivities: Coding side projects, design experiments, logic games, maker clubs
basicInfo.topicsCuriousAbout: Software products, AI systems, cybersecurity, startup ideas
basicInfo.personalStrengths: Pattern recognition, self-learning, focus, problem solving
basicInfo.avoidsOrDislikes: Heavy repetition without purpose, unclear communication, slow bureaucracy

questions.question: How do you react when pressure builds close to a deadline?
questions.whyItMatters: This measures emotional steadiness under stress.
options: I freeze or avoid the work.
I panic and lose quality.
I feel stress but can still finish the work.
I stay composed, prioritize, and keep moving.

questions.question: What do you usually do when someone gives you feedback?
questions.whyItMatters: This checks listening and response quality.
options: I take it personally and shut down.
I listen, but I rarely change anything.
I consider it if it seems useful.
I listen carefully, ask clarifying questions, and improve.

questions.question: In group work, when do you usually step forward?
questions.whyItMatters: This measures initiative and coordination tendency.
options: I prefer that someone else always leads.
I lead only when no one else wants to.
I can guide the group if needed.
I naturally organize people, clarify direction, and keep momentum.

questions.question: What happens when your first attempt fails?
questions.whyItMatters: This checks recovery after setbacks.
options: I usually give up quickly.
I feel upset and need a long time before trying again.
I recover after some thought and try another approach.
I learn from the setback, adjust fast, and continue.

questions.question: When instructions are unclear, what do you usually do first?
questions.whyItMatters: This checks whether the student seeks structure before acting.
options: I get stuck unless someone explains every step.
I try one thing quickly and hope it works.
I break the task into parts and test what seems most likely.
I clarify the goal, map the problem, and move in a structured way.

questions.question: If a role requires stamina plus mental focus at the same time, how do you react?
questions.whyItMatters: This checks combined physical and mental endurance.
options: That kind of pressure drains me very quickly.
I can do it briefly, but not consistently.
I can handle it with preparation.
I am mentally ready for demanding routines if the mission matters.

questions.question: How do you react when a team result depends partly on you?
questions.whyItMatters: This checks ownership under shared responsibility.
options: I avoid responsibility because I do not want the pressure.
I do only my own part and hope the rest works out.
I take responsibility for my part and support others if needed.
I actively coordinate, communicate, and protect the final outcome.

questions.question: If you need to explain an idea to others, how do you usually do it?
questions.whyItMatters: This reveals clarity and confidence in communication.
options: I avoid explaining because I am uncomfortable.
I speak, but people often leave confused.
I can explain clearly when I prepare.
I usually simplify ideas well and adjust for the audience.

questions.question: What feels most natural when you face a complex problem?
questions.whyItMatters: This reveals comfort with logic and patterns.
options: I avoid it if I can.
I try random solutions until one works.
I compare a few possibilities and choose one.
I look for patterns, causes, and evidence before deciding.

questions.question: How do you feel about work that may require long hours on your feet or active movement?
questions.whyItMatters: This measures comfort with physically demanding roles.
options: I strongly prefer to avoid it.
I can handle it rarely, but not often.
I can manage it if the work matters.
I am comfortable with active, physically demanding routines.

questions.question: How careful are you with small but important details?
questions.whyItMatters: This checks execution reliability.
options: I miss details often and do not revisit them.
I notice some details, but I rush a lot.
I usually review my work once before finishing.
I check carefully and take responsibility for getting it right.

questions.question: During a disagreement, what matters most to you?
questions.whyItMatters: This shows whether the student can respect other viewpoints.
options: Winning the argument as fast as possible.
Ending the discussion even if the other person feels unheard.
Finding a fair solution if possible.
Understanding both sides and helping the situation settle constructively.

analysis.summary: Nila Joseph shows stronger signs of Physical Stamina and Empathy. The current profile suggests promise, but also a need to keep developing Adaptability and Analytical.
analysis.characterReadout: This is a readiness-oriented interpretation based on how the student answered scenario-style questions and described their current interests.
workPreferences: May perform better with guidance, team rhythm, and visible support.
Open to physically active or field-heavy roles.

interestSignals.reason: Keywords in the profile point toward technology.
Keywords in the profile point toward science.
Keywords in the profile point toward creative.
Keywords in the profile point toward business.
Keywords in the profile point toward education.
No strong direct evidence yet for healthcare.

evaluation.narrative: Aarav currently shows strong readiness for Army Officer because his profile reflects discipline, stamina, and comfort with responsibility. The main growth area is becoming more emotionally ready for separation and high-risk service realities.
evaluation.parentSummary: Army Officer readiness is currently 84%. This suggests a strong fit for structured service, while the biggest family discussion should focus on emotional readiness for separation and long-term duty.
evaluation.schoolSummary: The student earned 840 proof points for Army Officer. School mentors can strengthen high-pressure decision-making, emotional steadiness, and realistic service preparation.
nextSteps: Give the student leadership roles that require calm decision-making under pressure.
Add reflective routines that build emotional steadiness around sacrifice and separation.
Expose the student to real service stories so readiness stays realistic, not romanticized.

evaluation.narrative: Nila currently shows strong readiness for Software Engineer. The strongest evidence appears in problem solving, self-direction, and willingness to keep learning as technology changes.
evaluation.parentSummary: Software Engineer readiness is currently 82%. The student already shows a strong technical mindset, and the main support need is building communication discipline for collaborative work.
evaluation.schoolSummary: The student earned 820 proof points for Software Engineer. Mentors can support growth by adding team-based projects, delivery routines, and feedback-heavy collaboration.
nextSteps: Keep the student in project-based work where problem solving is visible.
Build stronger communication habits through pair work and demos.
Repeat deadlines and delivery practice so discipline stays consistent under ambiguity.

evaluation.narrative: The student shows low readiness for Travel Consultant. Stronger evidence appears in Service and Adaptability, while Composure needs more deliberate support.
evaluation.parentSummary: Travel Consultant readiness is currently 15%. This score suggests low readiness. Parents should focus on helping the student strengthen Composure without discouraging progress in Service.
evaluation.schoolSummary: The student earned 150 proof points for Travel Consultant. School mentors can support improvement in Composure and Communication through guided experiences and reflective coaching.
nextSteps: Give the student a small real-world challenge that develops Composure.
Continue exposure projects that use Service.
Review the proof score again after one month of focused practice.
